10.1016/j.jmp.2018.07.002
Many psychological models can be characterized in terms of functions or mappings between sets of independent, latent, and dependent variables  (Dunn & Kalish, 2018) . In this characterization, an  input mapping  maps independent variables to a set of theory-dependent latent variables, and an  output mapping  maps the latent variables to a set of dependent variables. Let  
                         y 
                       be a vector of the values of  
                         n 
                       dependent variables, let  
                         x 
                       be a vector of the values of  
                         m 
                       latent variables, and  
                         w 
                       be a vector of the values of  
                         k 
                       independent variables. Then we can write the input mapping as,  
                         
                            
                               x 
                               = 
                               G

                                  w

                                  x

                               F

                               G

                         G

Models composed of structural and measurement mappings have been proposed in many fields, including judgment and choice  (Tversky & Russo, 1969) , risky decision making  (Kahneman & Tversky, 1979) , intertemporal choice  (Dai & Busemeyer, 2014) , and psychometric testing  (Andrich, 1988) . Signal detection theory (SDT) also offers examples of this kind of model  (Macmillan & Creelman, 2005)  which we use to illustrate the distinction. In the most basic SDT model, there are two dependent variables of interest; the  hit rate  (HR) or the probability of reporting a signal when one is present, and the  false alarm rate  (FAR) or the probability of reporting a signal when one is not present. Each dependent variable is modeled as a function of two latent variables; discriminability,  
                         
                            
                               d 
                            
                            
                               ′ 
                            
                         
                      , and a decision criterion,  
                         c 
                      . That is,

                                                 d

                                           c 
                                           )

It is clear that Eq.  (1)  can be decomposed into a structural mapping

SDA tests predictions of a model which do not require estimation of either the values of the latent variables or the form of the monotonic measurement mapping. Instead, predictions are based on geometric properties of the structural mapping that are invariant under all possible monotonically increasing transformations. It is in this sense that SDA tests for ‘structure under monotonicity’.

As we discuss in more detail in Section  3.4 , SDA can also be viewed as a generalization of  state-trace analysis  (STA), first introduced by  Bamber (1979) . As in SDA, STA tests a model that can be decomposed into a structural and measurement mapping where each component of the structural mapping is a function of a single latent variable (for further discussion, see  Dunn & Kalish, 2018; Dunn, Kalish, & Newell, 2014; Kalish, Dunn, Burdakov, & Sysoev, 2016; Loftus, Oberg, & Dillon, 2004; Newell & Dunn, 2008 ).

In their initial presentation of SDA,  Dunn and James (2003)  derived the relevant model predictions from first principles. However, a simpler, clearer, and more productive derivation can be obtained using the mathematical theory of  oriented matroids . The main aim of the present article is therefore to re-cast the theory of SDA in terms of oriented matroids and, based on this approach, to extend SDA in two directions that were discussed by  Dunn and James (2003)  without clear resolution. The first is the case in which two or more component functions of the measurement mapping are identical. This is a feature of many models including those derived from SDT as well as  additive conjoint measurement  (ACM)  (Krantz, Luce, Suppes, & Tversky, 1971; Luce & Tukey, 1964) . The second direction concerns the question of testing a model against data that contain measurement error. We discuss this in light of the procedure recently developed by  Kalish et al. (2016)  for STA.

The remainder of this article is divided into the following main sections. In Section  2 , we outline the aim of SDA in more precise terms. In Section  3 , we introduce the theory of oriented matroids in so far as it is relevant to SDA. In Section  4 , we use oriented matroids to derive testable consequences for models with a linear structural mapping. In Section  5 , we generalize this to a class of nonlinear models with ‘near linear’ structural mappings. In Section  6 , we derive additional testable consequences for linear structural models in which some or all components of the measurement mapping are identical (as in SDT and ACM). In Section  7 , we discuss the problem of testing a model in the presence of measurement error and conclude our discussion in Section  8 .

The aim of SDA is to provide a test of a psychological model in which the output mapping consists of a structural mapping and an unspecified but monotonically increasing measurement mapping. Let

The logic of SDA rests on the fact that it is possible to identify properties of the structural mapping  
                         g 
                       that survive arbitrary monotonic transformation. Specifically, the sign of the difference between two vectors of dependent variables observed under different experimental conditions is given by the sign of the difference between two corresponding vectors of pre-dependent variables. The set of such vectors, we show, is determined by geometric properties of  
                         g 
                      . Therefore, once a model has been defined in terms of its structural mapping, it can in principle be tested using SDA.

We show below that a structural mapping  
                         g 
                       partitions a set of sign vectors into a  permitted  set and a  forbidden  set. The permitted set consists of the closure (defined in Section  3.6 ) of the set of observable sign vectors, and the forbidden set is its complement. Any observed signed difference vector in the permitted set is consistent with  
                         g 
                      , while any observed signed difference vector in the forbidden set is inconsistent with  
                         g 
                      . This leads to the following in principle test of a model using SDA.

Let  
                         Y 
                         = 
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     y 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               … 
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     y 
                                  
                                  
                                     N 
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                       be the set of values of the dependent variable vector observed under  
                         N 
                       distinct experimental conditions. We say that  
                         f 
                         ∘ 
                         g 
                       is a  model  of  
                         Y 
                       for a given structural mapping  
                         g 
                      , if there exists a set of latent variable vectors,  
                         X 
                         = 
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               … 
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     N 
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                      , and a monotonically increasing measurement mapping  
                         f 
                      , such that  
                         
                            
                               y 
                            
                            
                               i 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         f 
                         ∘ 
                         g 
                         
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  x 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                       for all  
                         i 
                         ∈ 
                         
                            [ 
                            N 
                            ] 
                         
                      , where  
                         
                            [ 
                            N 
                            ] 
                         
                         = 
                         
                            { 
                            1 
                            , 
                            2 
                            , 
                            … 
                            , 
                            N 
                            } 
                         
                      . Let  
                         P 
                       be the set of permitted sign vectors associated with  
                         g 
                      . It follows that if  
                         f 
                         ∘ 
                         g 
                       is a model of  
                         Y 
                       then  
                         sign 
                         
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  y 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                            − 
                            
                               
                                  y 
                               
                               
                                  j 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                         ∈ 
                         P 
                       for all  
                         
                            
                               y 
                            
                            
                               i 
                            
                         
                         , 
                         
                            
                               y 
                            
                            
                               j 
                            
                         
                         ∈ 
                         Y 
                      . Conversely, if there exist any  
                         
                            
                               y 
                            
                            
                               i 
                            
                         
                         , 
                         
                            
                               y 
                            
                            
                               j 
                            
                         
                         ∈ 
                         Y 
                       such that  
                         sign 
                         
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  y 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                            − 
                            
                               
                                  y 
                               
                               
                                  j 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                         ∉ 
                         P 
                       then  
                         f 
                         ∘ 
                         g 
                       is  not  a model of  
                         Y 
                      . Consequently, the model is rejected for all possible values of the latent variables and for all possible monotonic measurement mappings.

A model consistent with Eq.  (2)  partitions the set of signed difference vectors into permitted and forbidden sets. For a linear structural mapping, these sets are defined with reference to its associated  oriented matroid , which is the focus of this section. In Section  4 , we define the permitted and forbidden sets of a linear structural mapping and extend this to varieties of nonlinear structural mappings in Section  5 .

An oriented matroid is a combinatorial object that describes the underlying structure common to several different kinds of mathematical objects, including arrangements of hyperplanes, directed graphs, point configurations, and (our primary interest) linear subspaces  (Björner, Las Vergnas, Sturmfels, White, & Ziegler, 1999) . This structure can be described in several different (but equivalent) ways, and different descriptions are convenient in different mathematical contexts.

The theory of oriented matroids is a well-developed area of combinatorics, but it is not widely known outside the world of discrete mathematics. For this reason, we provide a simple, and necessarily incomplete, introduction to the topic. To help build a geometric intuition, we begin by introducing an oriented matroid as a way of describing the relative position of points on a plane. We then link this to an equivalent description in terms of vector spaces. The key idea is that an oriented matroid can be defined by several different sets of sign vectors (which we discuss) which, in turn, form the basis of our distinction between permitted and forbidden sets. For a more detailed introduction, see  Björner et al. (1999) ,  Festa (2009) , and  Richter-Gebert and Ziegler (2004) .

One way an oriented matroid arises is as a description of a set of points in  
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                         . As an illustration, consider the set of four points,  
                            A 
                            ⊂ 
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                         , shown in  Fig. 1 . The position of each of these points in relation to the other points can be described by labeling it according to its position relative to different lines on the plane. One such line is shown in  Fig. 1 . Points on one side of this line are labeled positive  
                            
                               ( 
                               + 
                               ) 
                            
                         , points on the other side are labeled negative  
                            
                               ( 
                               − 
                               )

                               ( 
                               0 
                               ) 
                            
                         . In this way, each possible line is associated with a sign vector in the set,  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     { 
                                     0 
                                     , 
                                     + 
                                     , 
                                     − 
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  4 
                               
                            
                         , in which each component of which is associated, in order, with the points,  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                         ,  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                         ,  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  3 
                               
                            
                         , and  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  4 
                               
                            
                         . Accordingly, the line shown in  Fig. 1  is associated with the sign vector,  
                            
                               ( 
                               + 
                               , 
                               + 
                               , 
                               − 
                               , 
                               − 
                               )

                                  p

                          and

                               − 
                               , 
                               − 
                               , 
                               + 
                               , 
                               + 
                               )

                            1

The oriented matroid given by an arrangement of points is invariant under changes of scale, translation, rotation, and reflection, but not under a relabeling of the points. The analysis is also easily extended to higher dimensions. In general, for a set of points in  
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                         , we consider all possible  
                            
                               ( 
                               n 
                               − 
                               1 
                               ) 
                            
                         -dimensional affine hyperplanes that divide  
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                          into a positive  
                            
                               ( 
                               + 
                               ) 
                            
                          side and a negative  
                            
                               ( 
                               − 
                               ) 
                            
                          side.

While we have motivated the idea of an oriented matroid in terms of arrangements of points, our main focus will be on using oriented matroids to describe linear subspaces. Fortunately, the extension of what we have considered so far to subspaces is relatively straightforward. The set of points,  
                            A 
                         , shown in  Fig. 1  can also be represented in terms of their homogeneous coordinates. The homogeneous coordinates of a point in  
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  2

                               ( 
                               x 
                               , 
                               y 
                               )

In this section we show how an analysis based on oriented matroids is consistent with the logic of state-trace analysis (STA). As mentioned earlier, STA is a special case of SDA that tests a model in which each component of the structural mapping is a function of a single latent variable. Let  
                            
                               
                                  y 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  y

In this section, we present three principal (and equivalent) characterizations of an oriented matroid associated with a linear subspace. These are the sets of signed covectors (previously introduced),  signed vectors , and  signed circuits . They are formally defined as follows:

Let  
                            V 
                          be a linear subspace of  
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                          and let  
                            μ 
                            
                               ( 
                               V 
                               ) 
                            
                          be the oriented matroid of  
                            V 
                         . Then  
                            
                               
                                  V 
                               
                               
                                  ∗ 
                               
                            
                            ≔ 
                            
                               { 
                               sign 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  v 
                                  ) 
                               
                               : 
                               v 
                               ∈ 
                               V 
                               } 
                            
                          is the set of signed covectors of  
                            μ 
                            
                               ( 
                               V 
                               ) 
                            
                         . Let  
                            
                               
                                  V 
                               
                               
                                  ⊥ 
                               
                            
                          be the orthogonal complement of  
                            V 
                         . Then  
                            V 
                            ≔ 
                            
                               { 
                               sign 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  v 
                                  ) 
                               
                               : 
                               v 
                               ∈ 
                               
                                  
                                     V 
                                  
                                  
                                     ⊥ 
                                  
                               
                               } 
                            
                          is the set of signed vectors of  
                            μ 
                            
                               ( 
                               V 
                               ) 
                            
                         . Note that  
                            V 
                          is also the set of signed covectors of  
                            μ 
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     V 
                                  
                                  
                                     ⊥ 
                                  
                               
                               ) 
                            
                         . The set of signed circuits,  
                            C

                            V 
                         , is defined as the set of elements of  
                            V 
                            ∖ 
                            
                               { 
                               0 
                               }

                            2 
                          
                         
                            2

                         Geometrically,

                            3 
                          
                         
                            3 
                            In their treatment,  Dunn and James (2003)  used the negation of orthogonality, which they called  sign concordance . 
                         :

1. 
                               For each  
                                     i

For instance, if  
                            x 
                          and  
                            y 
                          are orthogonal vectors in  
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                         , then  
                            
                               
                                  ∑ 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                                  = 
                                  1 
                               
                               
                                  n

                                  i 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            0 
                         , and so either each term in the sum is 0 or this sum has both positive and negative terms. Thus  
                            sign 
                            
                               ( 
                               x 
                               ) 
                            
                            ⊥ 
                            sign 
                            
                               ( 
                               y 
                               ) 
                            
                         . For a set  
                            S 
                          of sign vectors ,  
                            
                               
                                  S 
                               
                               
                                  ⊥ 
                               
                            
                          is defined to be the set of sign vectors that are orthogonal to all elements of  
                            S 
                         . Every element of  
                            V 
                          is orthogonal to every element of  
                            
                               
                                  V 
                               
                               
                                  ⊥ 
                               
                            
                         , and thus  
                            
                               
                                  V 
                               
                               
                                  ∗ 
                               
                            
                            ⊆ 
                            
                               
                                  V 
                               
                               
                                  ⊥ 
                               
                            
                         . Remarkably, something stronger is true:  
                            
                               (4) 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        V 
                                     
                                     
                                        ∗ 
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        V 
                                     
                                     
                                        ⊥ 
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        C 
                                     
                                     
                                        ⊥ 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                         and likewise  
                            V 
                            = 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           V 
                                        
                                        
                                           ∗ 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ⊥ 
                               
                            
                         
                         (Björner et al., 1999) .

Thus, each of  
                            
                               
                                  V 
                               
                               
                                  ∗ 
                               
                            
                         ,  
                            V 
                         , and  
                            C 
                          carries the same information about  
                            V 
                         . To say this more formally:

                            V 
                         , whether expressed as  
                            
                               
                                  V 
                               
                               
                                  ∗

                            V 
                         , as  
                            C

                            μ 
                            
                               ( 
                               V 
                               )

                            V 
                         . An important but potentially confusing aspect of oriented matroids is that  
                            μ 
                            
                               ( 
                               V 
                               )

                                  V 
                               
                               
                                  ∗ 
                               
                            
                         ,  
                            V 
                         , or  
                            C

                            μ 
                            
                               ( 
                               V 
                               ) 
                            
                         . And even these do not exhaust the set of equivalent characterizations of  
                            μ 
                            
                               ( 
                               V 
                               ) 
                            
                          (for a comprehensive treatment, see  Björner et al., 1999 ).

When we consider multiple oriented matroids, we distinguish the signed covector set of an oriented matroid  
                            M 
                          by  
                            
                               
                                  V 
                               
                               
                                  ∗ 
                               
                            
                            
                               ( 
                               M 
                               ) 
                            
                         , and likewise,  
                            V 
                            
                               ( 
                               M 
                               ) 
                            
                          and  
                            C 
                            
                               ( 
                               M 
                               ) 
                            
                         .  Appendix B  outlines a method of constructing  
                            C 
                         , and hence  
                            V 
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  V 
                               
                               
                                  ∗ 
                               
                            
                         , given a linear subspace.

We digress briefly to mention the broader theory of oriented matroids. In general, oriented matroids are defined in purely combinatorial terms, without reference to  
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                         . That is, a  signed vector set  is defined to be a collection of sign vectors satisfying certain combinatorial axioms. We have not listed these axioms because they are not helpful in understanding the geometric intuition. Likewise,  signed circuit sets  have an abstract combinatorial definition, which is given in  Appendix A . The  
                            V 
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  V 
                               
                               
                                  ∗ 
                               
                            
                          arising from a subspace  
                            V 
                          of  
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                          are motivating examples for the general definition of signed vector sets, and likewise the  
                            C 
                          arising from  
                            V 
                          motivates the definition of signed circuit set. The fundamental result of oriented matroid theory is that these abstract combinatorial objects are related as in Eq.  (4) , and thus one can define an oriented matroid to be the information encoded by any one of these collections of sign vectors. An oriented matroid arising from a subspace  
                            V 
                          of  
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                          is said to be  realizable . But there are also signed vector sets and signed circuit sets which do not arise from subspaces of  
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                         , for which the resulting oriented matroid is  non-realizable .

It should be clear why we characterize  
                         
                            
                               V 
                            
                            
                               ↓ 
                            
                            
                               ∗ 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               C 
                            
                            
                               ↑ 
                            
                         
                       as permitted and forbidden, respectively. 
                         7 
                       
                      
                         7 
                         
                            Dunn and James (2003)  referred to permitted and forbidden sign vectors as  occupied  and  unoccupied zones , respectively. Their usage was, however, not always consistent. The present definition provides a more rigorous basis for the distinction. 
                      For linear structural mapping, it is in principle possible to find a pair of latent variable vectors such that the resulting observed signed difference vector is either an element of the set of signed covectors of the associated oriented matroid, or arbitrarily close to one. In contrast, it is never possible to find a pair of latent variable vectors such that the signed difference vector is an element of the forbidden set.

An example of a linear structure under monotonicity is provided by  Stephens, Dunn, and Hayes (2018) . They considered a set of 16 signal detection models of endorsement rates in an argument evaluation task. In this task, people are asked to evaluate deductively valid or invalid arguments under either  deduction  or  induction  instructions. Under deduction instructions, participants endorse an argument if the truth of the conclusion is judged to follow necessarily from the truth of the premises. Under induction instructions, an argument is endorsed if the conclusion is judged to be plausible given the truth of the premises. The combination of argument validity and instruction defines four dependent variables; the proportion of endorsements of deductively valid arguments under deduction instructions  
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     P 
                                  
                                  
                                     D 
                                     v 
                                  
                               
                               ) 
                            
                         , the proportion of endorsements of deductively invalid arguments under deduction instructions  
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     P 
                                  
                                  
                                     D 
                                     i 
                                  
                               
                               ) 
                            
                         , the proportion of endorsements of deductively valid arguments under induction instructions  
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     P 
                                  
                                  
                                     I 
                                     v 
                                  
                               
                               ) 
                            
                         , and the proportion of endorsements of deductively invalid arguments under induction instructions  
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     P 
                                  
                                  
                                     I 
                                     i 
                                  
                               
                               ) 
                            
                         .

It is currently debated whether performance on the argument evaluation task is best understood as involving one or two kinds of reasoning processes  (Evans & Stanovich, 2013; Keren & Schul, 2009) . Stephens et al. operationalized this theoretical distinction in terms of a set of signal detection models with different numbers of latent variables representing argument strength — one latent variable for ‘single-process’ models, and two latent variables for ‘dual-process’ models. One of the models considered by Stephens et al. was the following single-process model,

                            0

Thus, this model partitions the set of 81 possible sign vectors of four dependent variables into 57 permitted sign vectors and 24 forbidden sign vectors. In Section  7.1  we use this information to fit this model to data.

In many psychological models, the structural mapping is nonlinear. In this section, we examine such mappings and consider in detail a class of mapping, called  orthomestic , that are ‘near linear’ in the sense that they can be associated to an oriented matroid. In this, we largely follow the treatment given by  Dunn and James (2003) .

Let  
                         M 
                       be an oriented matroid with signed circuit set  
                         C 
                      . The rank of  
                         M 
                       is defined to be the smallest value  
                         m 
                       such that every element of  
                         C 
                       has support of size at most  
                         m 
                         + 
                         1 
                      . That is, if  
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     max 
                                  
                                  
                                     X 
                                     ∈ 
                                     C 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  ‖ 
                                  supp

                                     X 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  ‖ 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               m 
                               + 
                               1 
                               . 
                            
                         
                      If  
                         M 
                       is the oriented matroid associated to a vector space  
                         V 
                       then the rank of  
                         M 
                       coincides with the dimension of  
                         V 
                      .

                         g

We show in  Appendix C  by means of a counterexample that  Conjecture G.2  is refuted. It therefore remains an open question as to whether there exist other (simpler) means of establishing that a mapping is orthomestic.

In this section, we examine a mapping that can be shown to be orthomestic by  Proposition 5.2 .

In some applications of signal detection theory (e.g., so-called unequal variance models), a latent variable corresponding to the variance of the signal or target distribution is included. This can be minimally applied to the model proposed by  Stephens et al. (2018) , given by Eq.  (5) , to yield the following set of equations:

An application of  Problem 6  occurs in Problem 3 of  Dunn and James (2003) . As an aside, determining orthomesticity through the integrated Jacobian matrix in practice is often very difficult. It is for this reason that  Dunn and James (2003)  proposed  Conjecture G.2 . If this were true then it would have offered a simpler and more tractable way of showing that a nonlinear mapping is orthomestic.

In this section, we consider a linear structural model of the form  
                         y 
                         = 
                         f 
                         
                            ( 
                            A 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                      , in which some components of the dependent vector  
                         y 
                       behave similarly, in terms of both  
                         A 
                       and  
                         f 
                      , leading to additional testable constraints. Specifically, we examine models that have both of the following two properties:

1. 
                            a permutation of some or all of the components of  
                                  f 
                                leaves it unchanged, in which case we say that  
                                  f 
                                is  symmetric  under this permutation; and 
                         
                         
                            2. 
                            the same permutation of the rows of  
                                  A 
                                leaves the column space of  
                                  A 
                                unchanged, in which case we say that  
                                  A 
                                is  column space symmetric  under this permutation.

a permutation of some or all of the components of  
                                  f 
                                leaves it unchanged, in which case we say that  
                                  f 
                                is  symmetric  under this permutation; and

the same permutation of the rows of  
                                  A 
                                leaves the column space of  
                                  A 
                                unchanged, in which case we say that  
                                  A 
                                is  column space symmetric  under this permutation.

To illustrate these terms, consider the signal detection model first presented in Eq.  (1) . If  
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                       then the measurement mapping is symmetric under the permutation  
                         
                            ( 
                            2 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      . The structural mapping has the form,  
                         g 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         A 
                         x 
                      , where,  
                         
                            
                               A 
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              1 
                                           
                                           
                                              − 
                                              1 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           
                                              1 
                                           
                                           
                                              0 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                               . 
                            
                         
                      Permutation of the rows of  
                         A 
                       gives,  
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     A 
                                  
                                  
                                     ̃ 
                                  
                               
                               =

                         col 
                         
                            ( 
                            A

                                  A 
                               
                               
                                  ̃

                         A 
                       is column space symmetric under this permutation. 
                         8 
                       
                      
                         8 
                         This can be checked by converting  
                               A 
                             and  
                               
                                  
                                     A 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                             to their reduced column echelon forms. If these are identical then the column space is unchanged.

We explore the implications of these properties in detail with respect to the linear structure corresponding to the additive conjoint measurement (ACM) model and show that the additional testable constraints that arise are equivalent to the well-known cancellation conditions of ACM.

As a preliminary, we briefly review permutations and permutation matrices. A  permutation  of a set  
                            S 
                          is a bijection  
                            π 
                            : 
                            S 
                            → 
                            S 
                         . We will primarily be interested in permutations of the set

                               n

In Section  4.1 , we considered one of the 16 linear structural models proposed by  Stephens et al. (2018)  to account for endorsement rates of valid and invalid arguments under deduction and induction instructions. Another of the linear structural models they examined has the following matrix,  
                            
                               
                                  A 
                                  =

                                                 1

                         which is column space symmetric under the following eight permutations, including the identity permutation;  
                            
                               (

Recall that the dependent variables in this study are the proportions of endorsements of; (1) valid arguments and (2) invalid arguments under deduction instructions, and (3) valid arguments and (4) invalid arguments under induction instructions. The permutation,

                               4 
                               , 
                               1 
                               , 
                               2 
                               ) 
                            
                         , therefore corresponds to the hypothesis that the components of the measurement mapping corresponding to induction and deduction instructions (but not valid and invalid arguments) are identical.

Additive conjoint measurement (ACM) is an example of a linear structural mapping under symmetry. As normally presented, the ACM model proposes that a single dependent variable is a monotonic function of the sum of a set of latent variables, each of which corresponds to a scale value associated with a level of an independent variable (e.g.,  Krantz et al., 1971; Luce & Tukey, 1964; Michell, 2014 ).

We consider two independent variables,  
                            
                               
                                  x 
                               
                               
                                  A 
                               
                            
                          with levels in the set  
                            A 
                            = 
                            
                               { 
                               
                                  
                                     a 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     a 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               … 
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     a 
                                  
                                  
                                     m 
                                  
                               
                               } 
                            
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  x 
                               
                               
                                  B 
                               
                            
                          with levels in the set  
                            B 
                            = 
                            
                               { 
                               
                                  
                                     b 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     b 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               … 
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     b 
                                  
                                  
                                     n 
                                  
                               
                               } 
                            
                         . Let

                                  id

As preliminary evidence for  Conjecture 6.3 , we will show that any  
                            y 
                          satisfying these conditions satisfies two consequences of ACM –  Single Cancellation  and  Double Cancellation . The Single Cancellation Condition states that for every  
                            i 
                            , 
                            j 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  i 
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                            ,

                                        y 
                                     
                                     
                                        i

                                              ′ 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  > 
                                  
                                     
                                        y 
                                     
                                     
                                        j

                                              ′

                                        y

                                              j

                         The Double Cancellation Condition states that for every  
                            i

Theorem 6.4 
                            
                               If  
                               
                                  y 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           y 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                           , 
                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     … 
                                     , 
                                     
                                        
                                           y 
                                        
                                        
                                           m 
                                           , 
                                           n 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                                
                               satisfies Conditions 1 and 2 of  
                               
                                  Conjecture  6.3  
                               then  
                               
                                  y 
                                
                               satisfies the Single Cancellation and Double Cancellation Conditions.

If  
                               
                                  y 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           y 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                           , 
                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     … 
                                     , 
                                     
                                        
                                           y 
                                        
                                        
                                           m 
                                           , 
                                           n 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                                
                               satisfies Conditions 1 and 2 of  
                               
                                  Conjecture  6.3  
                               then  
                               
                                  y 
                                
                               satisfies the Single Cancellation and Double Cancellation Conditions.

Before proving  Theorem 6.4 , we make the following comment. In their original presentation of conjoint measurement,  Luce and Tukey (1964)  were interested in the theoretical question of when relations between quantities  
                            
                               
                                  y 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                                  , 
                                  j 
                               
                            
                          could be described by an ACM model. They showed this was possible given Single Cancellation, Double Cancellation, and other conditions which are not testable.  Scott (1964)  gave testable necessary and sufficient conditions for values  
                            
                               
                                  y 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                                  . 
                                  j 
                               
                            
                          to satisfy an ACM model. These conditions include Single Cancellation, Double Cancellation, and many additional cancellation conditions. A tedious proof (given in  Appendix D ) proves that when  
                            m 
                          and  
                            n 
                          are less than or equal to 3 then Single Cancellation and Double Cancellation imply ACM.  Appendix D  also includes an example showing that when  
                            m 
                            = 
                            n 
                            = 
                            4 
                         , Single Cancellation and Double Cancellation may be satisfied while ACM is not.  Conjecture 6.3  ventures a characterization of consistency with ACM in terms of oriented matroids rather than cancellation.

The result in  Appendix D , together with  Theorem 6.4 , gives us the following.

Corollary 6.5 
                            
                               For  
                               
                                  m 
                                  , 
                                  n 
                                  ≤ 
                                  3 
                                
                               a vector  
                               
                                  y 
                                
                               satisfies Conditions 1 and 2 of  
                               
                                  Conjecture  6.3  
                               if and only if  
                               
                                  y 
                                
                               is consistent with the ACM model.

For  
                               
                                  m 
                                  , 
                                  n 
                                  ≤ 
                                  3 
                                
                               a vector  
                               
                                  y 
                                
                               satisfies Conditions 1 and 2 of  
                               
                                  Conjecture  6.3  
                               if and only if  
                               
                                  y 
                                
                               is consistent with the ACM model.

It follows from  Lemma 6.2  that  
                            
                               
                                  A 
                               
                               
                                  m 
                                  , 
                                  n 
                               
                            
                          is column space symmetric under any composition of these types of permutations. We now prove  Theorem 6.4 .

Double Cancellation reduces to Single Cancellation when either  
                                  i 
                                  , 
                                  j 
                                  , 
                                  k 
                                are not distinct or  
                                  
                                     
                                        i 
                                     
                                     
                                        ′

                                        k 
                                     
                                     
                                        ′ 
                                     
                                  
                                are not distinct, so to see Double Cancellation it suffices to consider the case where  
                                  m 
                                  = 
                                  n 
                                  = 
                                  3 
                               .

We can illustrate the application of SDA to a linear structure under symmetry with the following simple case. Suppose that an experiment is conducted in which a dependent variable is observed under the factorial combination of two independent variables,  
                            
                               
                                  x 
                               
                               
                                  A 
                               
                            
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  x 
                               
                               
                                  B 
                               
                            
                         , each with two levels. This generates the following 2  
                            × 
                          2 structure for the observed vector,  
                            y 
                            =
