Stephens et al. (2018)  recently used Eq.  (22)  to fit the linear structural model given in Eq.  (5)  (among others) to a set of data collected by  Singmann and Klauer (2011)  and of the form described in Section  4.1 . These data consisted of endorsement ratings (on a scale 0 to 100) of each of four different dependent variables (valid and invalid arguments under induction and deduction instructions) across 10 conditions defined by different argument types. The mean endorsement ratings are shown in  Table 1  along with the fitted means for the linear structural model given by Eq.  (5)  and the nonlinear model given by Eq.  (8) . The fit of the linear structural model, corresponding to the value of function  
                            F 
                          in Eq.  (22) , was 24.7 while the fit of the nonlinear model was 14.6. The better fit of the nonlinear model is to be expected given that its permitted set is a superset of the permitted set of the linear structural model (see  Table 2 ).

Kalish et al. also described a statistical test of the goodness (more properly, badness) of fit of Eq.  (21)  based on a bootstrap re-sampling procedure. They called this a test of the  latent order  on the two (or more) dependent variables under monotonicity. However, the more general Eq.  (22)  does not fit a single order to each dependent variable. For this reason, we describe the corresponding statistical test as a test of the  latent structure  of two or more dependent variables under monotonicity. Specifically, it is a test of the hypothesis that the data are consistent with the permitted set derived from the specified structural mapping.  Fig. 5  shows the results of the tests of the previously defined linear and nonlinear models. The distributions are kernel density estimates of the fits of 10,000 bootstrap samples. In each case, the null hypothesis can be rejected at  
                            α 
                            = 
                            0 
                            . 
                            05 
                         .

The approach described above addresses the goal, discussed by  Dunn and James (2003) , of developing a ‘systematic treatment of measurement error within an SDA framework’ (p. 400). Dunn and James proposed a partial solution to this problem by constructing a set of tests on the differences between pairs of conditions wherever these corresponded to a forbidden signed difference vector. The main limitation of this approach is that it treats each forbidden signed difference vector independently rather than as part of the total configuration of points in  n -dimensional space. The present approach overcomes this problem.

Dunn and James (2003)  applied the test they had developed to a data set originally investigated by  Cheal and Lyon (1994) . These data consist of observations on three different measures of spatial attention; visual search, texture segregation and location precuing, obtained under ten different experimental conditions (for more details see  Dunn & James, 2003 ). The theoretical question of interest was whether the three measures reflected one or more than one latent variable, each corresponding to a potentially different kind of spatial attention. Dunn and James identified eight different linear structural models; a one-dimensional model in which one latent variable affected the three measures, six two-dimensional models reflecting different ways in which two latent variables could plausibly affect the three measures, and one three-dimensional model in which each measure reflected a unique latent variable. The last of these is saturated and so is untestable.

Dunn and James found that five of the seven testable models could be rejected using their test. We applied the test based on bootstrap sampling of the fit of Eq.  (22)  and found that the same five models could be rejected at  
                            α 
                            = 
                            0 
                            . 
                            05 
                         .

The aim of the present paper has been to re-establish the mathematical foundations of signed difference analysis as initially developed by  Dunn and James (2003) . In order to do this, we drew extensively on the theory of oriented matroids, which enabled us to address and to clarify a number of questions which had been raised but not completely resolved by  Dunn and James (2003) .

While  Dunn and James (2003)  developed SDA from first principles, we show how it can be connected to existing mathematical concepts. The theory of oriented matroids provides a basis for identifying the properties of a model – more properly, its structural mapping – that survive arbitrary monotonic transformation. This ‘structure under monotonicity’ conforms to the set of signed difference vectors that arise as signed covectors of the oriented matroid associated with the structural mapping of the model. It is this central insight that allows a model to be tested without having to specify the form of its measurement mapping. Because the usual role of this mapping is to link the structural (and theoretically relevant) component of the model to the observed data, it is often desirable to avoid a joint test of the structural mapping and what is often little more than an educated guess of the form of the measurement mapping.

Using the theory of oriented matroids, we were also able to develop a rigorous basis for the partition of the set of sign vectors by a model into permitted and forbidden subsets. The central idea is that if an observed sign difference vector is permitted then it is consistent with the model, while if it is forbidden then it is inconsistent, thereby refuting the model if it is statistically reliable. The issue that we highlighted in this regard is how to classify the set of sign vectors that are neither signed covectors nor signed vectors of the oriented matroid associated to the model. By definition, all signed covectors are permitted (as they can be observed, in principle) and all signed vectors are forbidden (as they cannot be observed, even in principle). We showed that the remaining set of sign vectors are elements of either the closure of the set of signed covectors,  
                         
                            
                               V 
                            
                            
                               ↓ 
                            
                            
                               ∗ 
                            
                         
                      , or its complement,  
                         
                            
                               C 
                            
                            
                               ↑ 
                            
                         
                      . The elements of the former are formed by setting one or more nonzero components of a signed covector to zero. Geometrically, the orthants corresponding to these sign vectors lie at the boundaries of orthants associated with signed covectors and are thus each infinitesimally close to a permitted difference vector. For this reason, we classify the associated sign vectors as permitted. Alternatively, if one or more components of the measurement mapping is weakly, rather than strictly, increasing, difference vectors that lie in orthants associated with signed covectors of the associated oriented matroid will be forced to a boundary of this orthant by the measurement mapping. This is another reason to classify the associated sign vectors as permitted.

Our arguments in this regard are similar to those presented by  Wagenmakers, Krypotos, Criss, and Iverson (2012)  in their discussion of ‘removable’ and ‘non-removable’ interactions. The former are consistent with an underlying additive model while the latter are not. In a factorial design involving two independent variables each with two levels, a ‘non-removable’ interaction corresponds to a cross-over interaction. In the present paper, we analyzed this design in terms of a linear structural model under symmetry. In Section  6.5  we examined the 2  
                         × 
                       2 case and showed that data conforming to a cross-over interaction must produce one or more forbidden sign vectors under appropriate permutations. However, as highlighted by  Wagenmakers et al. (2012) , it is difficult to classify interactions as removable or non-removable when the difference between one or more conditions is zero. In his original discussion of this issue,  Loftus (1978)  classified these ‘almost’ cross-over interactions as non-removable (or, in his terminology, as interpretable). However, Wagenmakers et al. argued that it is in practice impossible to prove that a difference is exactly zero (for a similar discussion, see  Dunn, 2003 ) and classified these interactions as ‘borderline non-removable’. In our terms, the signed differences that arise from these data are elements of the closure of the set of signed covectors and, based on the arguments presented above, we classify them as permitted. They are thus also removable.

The foregoing analysis also applies to 2  
                         × 
                       3 and 3  
                         × 
                       3 factorial designs. In the latter case, violations of the additive model may arise due to failure of the Double Cancellation condition which, unlike failure of Single Cancellation, does not necessarily imply a cross-over interaction. We have proven that such violations of the additive model can be detected using SDA under appropriate permutations of the data.

In their original presentation of SDA, Dunn and James briefly discussed the relationship between SDA and other approaches such as STA, SDT, and ACM. Our treatment of SDA allowed us to draw firmer conclusions in this regard. First, we showed that STA is a special case of SDA in which each component of the composite structural and measurement mapping is a function of the same (single) latent variable. Therefore, without loss of generality, each component of the structural mapping can be interpreted as the identity mapping. Similarly, many SDT models can be tested using SDA. When the structural mapping is linear, it can each be associated to an oriented matroid.  Stephens et al. (2018)  tested a set of 16 SDT models of four dependent variables in this way. In addition, many nonlinear SDT models may also be tested using SDA if they can be shown to be orthomestic.

An orthomestic model is associated to a single oriented matroid on a specified domain. This can be demonstrated in two main ways: first, by showing that the Jacobian matrix of the structural mapping of the model is constant for all points in the domain; and, failing that, by showing that the integral Jacobian matrix of the structural mapping is associated to the same oriented matroid for all pairs of points in the domain. Analysis of the integral Jacobian is frequently very difficult if not intractable, which places limits on its usefulness. For this reason,  Dunn and James (2003)  conjectured that it may be sufficient to demonstrate that the Jacobian matrix of the structural mapping, while not constant, is associated to the same oriented matroid at all points in the domain. The value of this conjecture, if it were true, is that it would greatly simplify the analysis of nonlinear structural models to determine orthomesticity. However, by generating a counter-example, we showed that this conjecture is false.

We also showed that some ACM models can be tested using SDA by making use of symmetry relations inherent in the model. This forms part of a general procedure that can be employed whenever two or more dependent variables are associated with identical measurement functions. In the case of ACM, we showed that for factorial designs up to 3  
                         × 
                       3, the permuted form of SDA is equivalent to tests of Single and Double Cancellation. We conjecture that SDA is equivalent to higher order cancellation conditions for larger factorial designs as, for example, in a 4  
                         × 
                       4 design, where single, double, and triple cancellation conditions are required  (Michell, 2014) .

Finally, we addressed the question of measurement error and proposed a general solution based on the approach outlined by  Kalish et al. (2016) . This approach can be used to fit a model to data where it is defined only in terms of its structural mapping. Using bootstrap resampling, the statistical significance of this fit can be determined. In its present formulation, this approach assumes that each component of the measurement mapping is distinct. We speculate that it may be possible to extend this methodology to incorporate symmetry of the measurement mapping. If this is possible, then this would offer a means to fit data under symmetric models such as ACM  (Luce & Narens, 1994) .

 (Discovery Grant  DP130101535 ) without which the present work would not have been possible. We also thank Don Bamber and an anonymous reviewer for their extensive and helpful comments.

Dunn and James (2003)  conjectured that this condition on the Jacobian is both necessary and sufficient to guarantee orthomesticity. That is,

Conjecture G.2 
                            
                               Dunn and James (2003)  
                               . Let  
                               
                                  g 
                                  : 
                                  P 
                                  → 
                                  Q 
                                
                               be a mapping such that  
                               
                                  P

                                  g 
                                
                               exists on  
                               
                                  P 
                                
                               and is continuous. If the oriented matroid associated with  
                               
                                  col 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     D 
                                     g 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        a 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                                
                               is  
                               
                                  M 
                                
                               for all  
                               
                                  a 
                                  ∈ 
                                  P 
                               
                               , then  
                               
                                  g 
                                
                               is orthomestic with oriented matroid  
                               
                                  M 
                               
                               .

Below is a lemma that makes it relatively easy to construct counterexamples. Essentially, it says that if we find a curve in  
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  3 
                               
                            
                          and a rank 2 oriented matroid  
                            M 
                          such that the tangents to the curve all lie in rank 2 subspaces with oriented matroid  
                            M 
                         , then we can extend the curve to a 2-dimensional ribbon whose tangent planes all have oriented matroid  
                            M 
                         .

For convenience we will consider the rank 2 oriented matroid

                                  −

                               }

Proposition D.1 
                         
                            Let

Proof 
                         Assume without loss of generality that  
                               
                                  | 
                                  A 
                                  | 
                               
                               ≤ 
                               
                                  | 
                                  B 
                                  | 
                               
                            . If  
                               A 
                               = 
                               
                                  { 
                                  a 
                                  } 
                               
                             then we define  
                               u 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  a 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               0 
                             and, for each  
                               j 
                               ∈ 
                               B 
                            , define  
                               v 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  j 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     y 
                                  
                                  
                                     a 
                                     , 
                                  
                               
                            . Also define  
                               F 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               x 
                             for all  
                               x 
                            . 
                         Beyond this case, note: 
                         
                            
                               
                                  1. 
                                  Without loss of generality we may assume  
                                        
                                           
                                              y 
                                           
                                           
                                              i 
                                              , 
                                              
                                                 
                                                    i 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    ′ 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        ≥ 
                                        0 
                                      for each  
                                        i 
                                      and  
                                        
                                           
                                              i 
                                           
                                           
                                              ′ 
                                           
                                        
                                     . (If this is not true, simply choose a sufficiently large real number,  
                                        C 
                                     , and define  
                                        
                                           
                                              y 
                                           
                                           
                                              i 
                                              , 
                                              
                                                 
                                                    i 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    ′ 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                           
                                              ′

                                              i 
                                              , 
                                              
                                                 
                                                    i 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    ′ 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        + 
                                        C 
                                      for all  
                                        i 
                                      and  
                                        
                                           
                                              i 
                                           
                                           
                                              ′ 
                                           
                                        
                                     .) 
                               
                               
                                  2. 
                                  If  
                                        
                                           
                                              i 
                                           
                                           
                                              ′ 
                                           
                                        
                                      and  
                                        
                                           
                                              j

                                        B

                                      for some  
                                        i

                                              , 
                                              
                                                 
                                                    i

                                      for all  
                                        j 
                                     . Thus if we can find suitable functions  
                                        u 
                                     ,  
                                        v 
                                     , and  
                                        F 
                                      for the restricted problem on  
                                        A 
                                      and  
                                        B 
                                        ∖ 
                                        
                                           { 
                                           
                                              
                                                 j 
                                              
                                              
                                                 ′ 
                                              
                                           
                                           } 
                                        
                                     , then we can solve the full problem by defining  
                                        u 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           
                                              
                                                 j 
                                              
                                              
                                                 ′ 
                                              
                                           
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        = 
                                        u 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           
                                              
                                                 i 
                                              
                                              
                                                 ′ 
                                              
                                           
                                           ) 
                                        
                                     . Similar considerations hold when there are elements  
                                        i 
                                        , 
                                        j 
                                        ∈ 
                                        A 
                                      and  
                                        
                                           
                                              i 
                                           
                                           
                                              ′ 
                                           
                                        
                                        ∈ 
                                        B 
                                      so that  
                                        
                                           
                                              y 
                                           
                                           
                                              i 
                                              , 
                                              
                                                 
                                                    i 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    ′

                                              y 
                                           
                                           
                                              j 
                                              , 
                                              
                                                 
                                                    i 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    ′ 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     . Thus we will assume that  
                                        
                                           
                                              y 
                                           
                                           
                                              i 
                                              , 
                                              
                                                 
                                                    i 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    ′

                                              ,

                                                    ′

                                              ,

                                                    ′ 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        ≠ 
                                        
                                           
                                              y 
                                           
                                           
                                              j 
                                              , 
                                              
                                                 
                                                    i 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    ′ 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                      for all  
                                        i 
                                     ,  
                                        j 
                                     ,  
                                        
                                           
                                              i 
                                           
                                           
                                              ′ 
                                           
                                        
                                      and  
                                        
                                           
                                              j 
                                           
                                           
                                              ′ 
                                           
                                        
                                     .

                                  If we can find functions  
                                        u 
                                        : 
                                        A 
                                        → 
                                        R 
                                      and  
                                        v 
                                        : 
                                        B 
                                        → 
                                        R 
                                      such that, for all  
                                        i 
                                        , 
                                        
                                           
                                              i 
                                           
                                           
                                              ′ 
                                           
                                        
                                        , 
                                        j 
                                        , 
                                        
                                           
                                              j 
                                           
                                           
                                              ′ 
                                           
                                        
                                     ,  
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                    y 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    i 
                                                    , 
                                                    
                                                       
                                                          i 
                                                       
                                                       
                                                          ′ 
                                                       
                                                    
                                                 
                                              
                                              < 
                                              
                                                 
                                                    y 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    j 
                                                    , 
                                                    
                                                       
                                                          j 
                                                       
                                                       
                                                          ′ 
                                                       
                                                    
                                                 
                                              
                                              ⇔ 
                                              u 
                                              
                                                 ( 
                                                 i 
                                                 ) 
                                              
                                              + 
                                              v 
                                              
                                                 ( 
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       i 
                                                    
                                                    
                                                       ′ 
                                                    
                                                 
                                                 ) 
                                              
                                              < 
                                              u 
                                              
                                                 ( 
                                                 j 
                                                 ) 
                                              
                                              + 
                                              v 
                                              
                                                 ( 
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       j 
                                                    
                                                    
                                                       ′ 
                                                    
                                                 
                                                 ) 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     then we can complete the proof by first defining  
                                        F 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           u 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              i 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           + 
                                           v 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              
                                                 
                                                    i 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    ′ 
                                                 
                                              
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        = 
                                        
                                           
                                              y 
                                           
                                           
                                              i 
                                              , 
                                              
                                                 
                                                    i 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    ′ 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                      for each  
                                        i 
                                      and  
                                        
                                           
                                              i 
                                           
                                           
                                              ′ 
                                           
                                        
                                     , extending  
                                        F 
                                      linearly between any two successive values  
                                        u 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           i 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        + 
                                        v 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           
                                              
                                                 i 
                                              
                                              
                                                 ′ 
                                              
                                           
                                           ) 
                                        
                                     , and extending  
                                        F 
                                      by any continuous monotonically increasing function on all remaining real numbers. Thus for the remainder of the proof we will focus on finding suitable functions  
                                        u 
                                      and  
                                        v 
                                     . 
                               
                            
                         
                         If  
                               A 
                               = 
                               
                                  { 
                                  
                                     
                                        a 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        a 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                                  } 
                               
                             then without loss of generality  
                               
                                  
                                     y 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           a 
                                        
                                        
                                           1
